hero.tagline: Ang hindi marunong lumingon sa pinanggalingan ay hindi makararating sa paroroonan.
hero.translation: He who does not look back at where he came from will never reach his destination.

biography.overview: José Protasio Rizal Mercado y Alonso Realonda was a Filipino nationalist, writer, and polymath. The most prominent advocate for reform in the Philippines during the Spanish colonial era, he is regarded as the national hero of the Philippines.
funFacts: Rizal learned the alphabet at age 3 and could read and write fluently by age 5.
He was a sculptor, painter, poet, ophthalmologist, and nationalist — all at once.
His final poem, Mi Último Adiós, was hidden inside an oil lamp before his execution.
He completed his ophthalmology training and restored his mother's failing eyesight.
Rizal was only 35 years old when he was executed.
He practiced fencing and pistol shooting extensively while studying in Europe.
Rizal corresponded with European scholars including Ferdinand Blumentritt.
He designed and built a water system for the community in Dapitan.
His novel Noli Me Tángere was banned immediately upon arriving in the Philippines.

timeline.detail: José Rizal was born on June 19, 1861, the seventh of eleven children to Francisco Rizal Mercado II and Teodora Alonso Realonda.

timeline.event: Enrolled at the University of Santo Tomas
timeline.detail: Rizal studied Philosophy and Letters at UST, later shifting to Medicine — motivated by his mother Teodora's failing eyesight.
Secretly and without his parents' knowledge, Rizal sailed to Spain to continue his medical studies at the Universidad Central de Madrid.
Rizal completed his Licentiate in Medicine and began studying ophthalmology under Dr. Louis de Wecker in Paris.
Rizal's debut novel was published in Berlin, Germany, exposing the abuses of the Spanish colonial government and the Catholic Church.
Rizal toured Japan, the United States, and England, conducting research at the British Museum and annotating Morga's Sucesos de las Islas Filipinas.
The sequel to Noli was published in Ghent, Belgium. Darker and more revolutionary in tone, it urged more radical reform.
Upon returning to the Philippines, Rizal founded La Liga Filipina, a civic organization. He was arrested and exiled to Dapitan days later.
Rizal was arrested, tried by a military court, and found guilty of rebellion, sedition, and conspiracy — charges he denied.
On December 30, 1896, at 35 years old, Rizal was executed by musketry. His last poem, Mi Último Adiós, was secretly folded inside an oil lamp.

gallery.title: José Rizal Portrait
gallery.description: Official portrait of José Rizal, the Philippine national hero.

gallery.title: Noli Me Tángere — First Edition Cover
gallery.description: The cover of the first edition of Noli Me Tángere, published in Berlin in 1887.

gallery.title: Fort Santiago, Manila
gallery.description: The historical fortress where Rizal was imprisoned before his execution.

family.name: Francisco Rizal Mercado
family.description: A prosperous tenant farmer who leased hacienda land from the Dominicans in Calamba. A man of deep faith and quiet strength, he ensured all eleven children received the finest education available — a sacrifice that shaped José's destiny.
family.note: Inspired the character of Don Rafael Ibarra in Noli Me Tángere.

family.name: Teodora Alonso Realonda
family.description: An exceptionally educated woman for her era — she read Latin, managed the family finances, and was José's first teacher. Her failing eyesight drove Rizal to specialize in ophthalmology. He later successfully operated on her eyes in Dapitan.
family.note: The single greatest intellectual influence on young José.

family.name: Saturnina Rizal
family.description: Known as 'Neneng,' she was the eldest of the eleven siblings and a steady anchor for José throughout his life. Her husband Manuel Hidalgo was later exiled by the Spanish authorities because of Rizal's novels.
family.note: Closest sibling confidante during Rizal's early years.

family.name: Paciano Rizal
family.description: The only brother among the siblings and Rizal's greatest male ally. A revolutionary in his own right, Paciano financially supported José's studies in Europe and later became a general in the Philippine-American War. He was José's father figure, protector, and closest confidant.
family.note: Called by historians 'the other Rizal' — equally brave, less celebrated.

family.name: Narcisa Rizal
family.description: Known as 'Sisa' — the very name Rizal gave to the tragic mother in Noli Me Tángere. Narcisa was the one who secretly recovered José's remains after his execution and had them buried at Paco Cemetery, defying Spanish authorities.
family.note: Her nickname 'Sisa' immortalized in Noli Me Tángere.

family.name: Olimpia Rizal
family.description: Known as 'Ypia,' Olimpia died young at 32 — the same year Rizal published Noli Me Tángere. Her early death deeply saddened the family. She was married to Silvestre Ubaldo, a telegraph operator.
family.note: Died the same year Noli Me Tángere was published, 1887.

family.name: Lucia Rizal
family.description: Known as 'Uling,' Lucia married Mariano Herbosa, a nephew of Father Burgos. Because of his connection to Rizal, Herbosa was denied a Christian burial by the Church upon his death — a direct act of ecclesiastical vengeance.
family.note: Her husband was denied Christian burial due to Rizal's infamy.

family.name: Maria Rizal
family.description: Known as 'Biang,' Maria was the sibling closest in age to José before him. She married Daniel Faustino Cruz of Biñan and lived a relatively quiet life, though she shared in the family's suffering under Spanish scrutiny.
family.note: Immediately preceded José in birth order — his closest sister in age.

family.name: Concepcion Rizal
family.description: Known as 'Concha,' she was the sibling born just after José and tragically died at the age of three. Her death was the first great sorrow of young José's life, and he later wrote of weeping bitterly at her passing.
family.note: Her death at age 3 was Rizal's first encounter with grief and loss.

family.name: Josefa Rizal
family.description: Known as 'Panggoy,' Josefa suffered from epilepsy throughout her life and never married. She lived the longest among the siblings who survived into the 20th century and remained close to Trinidad in their later years.
family.note: Outlived José by nearly 50 years, passing away in 1945.

family.name: Trinidad Rizal
family.description: The keeper of Rizal's final poem, Mi Último Adiós. On the morning of his execution, Rizal slipped her a small oil lamp containing the hidden poem, whispering: 'There is something inside.' She preserved it for posterity and became the family's living memory.
family.note: Guardian of Mi Último Adiós — Rizal's farewell poem to the Philippines.

family.name: Soledad Rizal
family.description: Known as 'Choleng,' Soledad was the youngest of the Rizal children. She married Pantaleon Quintero. Though the youngest, she too bore the weight of the family name under Spanish colonial persecution following José's execution.
family.note: The youngest of eleven — the family's final gift.

works.title: Noli Me Tángere
works.subtitle: Touch Me Not
works.summary: A scathing social novel that exposes the corruption of Spanish colonial rule, the abuses of the Catholic friars, and the suffering of the Filipino people through the eyes of Crisostomo Ibarra, a young Filipino idealist who returns from Europe.
works.significance: The novel ignited a sense of Filipino national identity and is credited with sparking the revolution against Spain.

characters.name: Crisostomo Ibarra
characters.description: An idealistic young Filipino ilustrado who returns from Europe with dreams of reform, only to be crushed by the colonial system.

characters.name: María Clara
characters.description: Ibarra's childhood sweetheart, the epitome of the tragic Filipino woman — beautiful, devout, and imprisoned by social expectations.

characters.name: Padre Dámaso
characters.description: An arrogant and licentious Spanish friar who embodies the corruption and hypocrisy of the colonial Church.

characters.name: Elías
characters.description: A mysterious outlaw who represents the revolutionary class — those who have lost everything to injustice and demand radical change.

characters.name: Sisa
characters.description: A mother who goes mad searching for her sons Basilio and Crispin, symbolizing the tragedy of ordinary Filipinos victimized by colonial abuse.

works.title: El Filibusterismo
works.subtitle: The Reign of Greed
works.dedication: To GOMBURZA — the three martyred Filipino priests
works.summary: The darker, more revolutionary sequel to Noli. Ibarra, now disguised as the wealthy jeweler Simoun, returns to the Philippines not to reform the system, but to destroy it — plotting a violent revolution fueled by personal vendetta and nationalist rage.
works.significance: El Filibusterismo is more pessimistic than Noli, suggesting that peaceful reform is impossible and that Filipinos must awaken — or be destroyed by their own passivity.

characters.name: Simoun / Ibarra
characters.description: Crisostomo Ibarra reborn as a cynical, vengeful jeweler. He manipulates the colonial elite to fund a violent revolution, having abandoned his faith in peaceful reform.

characters.name: Basilio
characters.description: Sisa's son, now a medical student. He represents the educated youth torn between Simoun's violent path and the hope of a better future through education.

characters.name: Isagani
characters.description: A passionate poet and student who believes in reform through love and education, ultimately sabotaging Simoun's bomb plot out of love for Paulita.

characters.name: Padre Florentino
characters.description: A wise Filipino secular priest who serves as the novel's moral compass, delivering Rizal's famous message about freedom at the story's end.

characters.name: Cabesang Tales
characters.description: A hard-working farmer dispossessed of his land by the friars. His transformation into a bandit illustrates how injustice breeds lawlessness.

travels.detail: Rizal enrolled at the Universidad Central de Madrid, earning his Licentiate in Medicine. He became involved with Filipino expatriates and wrote for La Solidaridad.
Rizal studied ophthalmology under Dr. Otto Becker and had Noli Me Tángere published in Berlin at his own expense.
He trained under Dr. Louis de Wecker, one of Europe's greatest eye surgeons, further specializing in the craft he would use to restore his mother's sight.
At the British Museum, Rizal annotated Antonio de Morga's Sucesos de las Islas Filipinas, aiming to prove that pre-colonial Filipinos had a rich civilization.
Rizal spent time in Japan, fell briefly in love with O-Sei-San, and was captivated by Japanese culture, seeing parallels to Filipino resilience.
Rizal had El Filibusterismo printed in Ghent. Valentin Ventura financed the printing of the last few chapters when Rizal ran out of funds.
Rizal passed through the United States and documented the discrimination against Chinese immigrants, drawing parallels to colonial conditions in the Philippines.
Exiled by the Spanish government, Rizal spent four years in Dapitan. He practiced medicine, taught children, built infrastructure, and conducted scientific research.

group.courseName: GEM 001 — Life and Works of Rizal
group.motto: We don't just study Rizal. We build like him.

members.name: Harold Bermudez
members.description: The sole architect behind this entire website. Built the data pipeline, UI, animations, and all dynamic JS logic from scratch.

members.name: Fraise Vercille Abe
members.description: Coordinated the team's research efforts and kept the project on track.

members.name: Justin Hio
members.description: Gathered and verified historical facts about Rizal's life, works, and travels used throughout the site.

members.name: Mark Daniel Asilo
members.description: Contributed research on Rizal's literary works and characters, ensuring accuracy in every detail.

members.name: Renz Andrei Medrick Lim
members.description: Researched Rizal's travels and timeline of life events featured in the Biography section.

members.name: Tyler Hernandez
members.description: Compiled data on Rizal's novels, themes, and major characters integrated into the Literary Works section.

sources.title: Noli Me Tángere
sources.author: José Rizal
sources.detail: Berlin, Germany, 1887. Rizal's first novel exposing Spanish colonial abuses.

sources.title: El Filibusterismo
sources.author: José Rizal
sources.detail: Ghent, Belgium, 1891. The revolutionary sequel dedicated to GOMBURZA.

sources.title: Mi Último Adiós
sources.author: José Rizal
sources.detail: Written on the eve of his execution, December 29, 1896. Hidden inside an oil lamp.

sources.title: José Rizal: Life, Works and Writings of a Genius, Writer, Scientist and National Hero
sources.author: Gregorio F. Zaide & Sonia M. Zaide
sources.detail: All Nations Publishing Co., 1999. The standard academic reference for Rizal studies in Philippine universities.

sources.title: The First Filipino: A Biography of José Rizal
sources.author: León Ma. Guerrero
sources.detail: National Historical Commission of the Philippines, 1963. Considered the definitive English-language biography of Rizal.

sources.title: Rizal Without the Overcoat
sources.author: Ambeth R. Ocampo
sources.detail: Anvil Publishing, 1990. A collection of essays humanizing Rizal beyond the national hero myth.

sources.title: Sucesos de las Islas Filipinas
sources.author: Antonio de Morga (annotated by José Rizal)
sources.detail: Originally published 1609; annotated by Rizal in London, 1889. Used to prove pre-colonial Filipino civilization.

sources.title: National Historical Commission of the Philippines
sources.author: NHCP
sources.detail: nhcp.gov.ph — Official government body preserving Philippine historical records including Rizal's life and monuments.

sources.title: Rizal Shrine, Calamba, Laguna
sources.author: National Parks Development Committee
sources.detail: Birthplace of José Rizal, now a national shrine. Referenced for biographical details of Rizal's early life.

sources.title: Fort Santiago, Intramuros, Manila
sources.author: Intramuros Administration
sources.detail: The fortress where Rizal was imprisoned before his execution on December 30, 1896.

sources.title: Rizal Memorial, Luneta Park (Bagumbayan), Manila
sources.author: National Parks Development Committee
sources.detail: Site of Rizal's execution and home of the Rizal Monument, a UNESCO tentative World Heritage Site.

sources.title: Dapitan City — Rizal Shrine
sources.author: City Government of Dapitan
sources.detail: Site of Rizal's exile (1892–1896), where he practiced medicine, built infrastructure, and taught the community.

sources.title: José Rizal — Encyclopædia Britannica
sources.author: Encyclopædia Britannica Editors
sources.detail: britannica.com — Peer-reviewed encyclopedia entry covering Rizal's life, works, and legacy.

sources.title: José Rizal — Wikipedia
sources.author: Wikimedia Contributors
sources.detail: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/José_Rizal — General reference used for cross-checking dates, languages, and travel details.

sources.title: Universidad Central de Madrid — Historical Records
sources.author: Universidad Complutense de Madrid
sources.detail: ucm.es — Institutional records referenced for Rizal's medical studies in Spain (1882–1885).

sources.title: British Museum — Research & Collections
sources.author: The British Museum
sources.detail: britishmuseum.org — Referenced for Rizal's research visit to London (1888–1889) and his annotation of Morga's work.

media.title: Jose Rizal (1998) — Full Movie
media.description: The acclaimed Filipino biographical film starring Cesar Montano as José Rizal. Directed by Marilou Diaz-Abaya.

media.title: Jose Rizal: Sa Landas ng Paglaya
media.description: Official 35-minute documentary by the National Historical Commission of the Philippines covering Rizal's life, works, and heroic deeds.

media.title: Mi Último Adiós — Rizal's Farewell Poem (Spanish Language)
media.description: A dramatic reading and analysis of Rizal's final poem, written on the eve of his execution on December 29, 1896.

media.title: El Filibusterismo — Summary of the Entire Novel
media.description: Full breakdown of El Filibusterismo — Rizal's darker, more revolutionary second novel dedicated to GOMBURZA.

media.title: Rizal's Exile in Dapitan
media.description: Official documentary covering Rizal's four-year exile in Dapitan, Zamboanga — where he practiced medicine, taught, and built a water system.

media.title: Noli Me Tangere — Story
media.description: Deep dive into Noli Me Tángere — the novel that exposed Spanish colonial abuse and ignited Filipino national consciousness.
